text.title: Consumer Contract Tests Generated from OpenAPI / Traffic Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules grounding the test-consumer-contract-from-spec methodology plus the `skip-this-methodology` safety rule. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Consumer Pact files MUST be produced by a deterministic generator (MCP server, skill, or script) reading the canonical artifact. Hand-edited Pact JSON is rejected at PR time.
rule.rationale: Hand-written Pact drifts within weeks; generation pins it to the spec.

rule.statement: The generator MUST cite the source artifact by stable URI (Git SHA, OpenAPI commit hash, or traffic-capture file hash). Regenerating from a different source requires a new commit.
rule.rationale: Without a pinned source, can-i-deploy passes today and fails tomorrow because someone regenerated against `latest`.

rule.statement: The generator MUST emit both the Pact file AND a matching client test in the consumer's test framework. PR is rejected if either is missing.
rule.rationale: Pact without a client test means nothing exercises the contract; both must travel together.

rule.statement: `can-i-deploy` MUST be a required CI check on the provider repo. Merging a provider PR that breaks a published consumer contract is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Generation alone doesn't prevent breakage; the broker check is what makes contracts load-bearing.

rule.statement: Once committed, Pact files are read-only outputs. Modifying them in a feature branch (other than via regeneration from a new source) is rejected.
rule.rationale: Manual tweaks to generated files are the entry point for the drift this methodology exists to prevent.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the preconditions in `## Applies If` are not all true, or any item in `## Skip If` holds. The decision tree routes to this rule on those branches.
rule.rationale: A methodology that runs on the wrong input wastes tokens and produces misleading artifacts. The explicit skip rule is the safety valve.
